# Simulated playtest results

*Generated by `tools/playtest-sim.js`. 30 trials per tier, fixed 60 Hz tick, bots
restricted to the same virtual controls (and therefore the same paddle speed) a human has.*

---

## Rung 3 — Breakout — 2026-07-18

| | Beginner | Intermediate | Adept |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cleared wall 1 | **0%** | 67% | 100% |
| Won (both walls) | 0% | 40% | **100%** |
| Median score | **5** | 826 | **896 (perfect)** |
| Score range (p25–p75) | 4–7 | 443–896 | 896–896 |
| Time to clear wall 1 | never | **311 s** | **309 s** |
| Full game length | 16 s | 529 s | 603 s |
| Balls lost | 3 | 3 | **0** |

Whole sweep — 90 full games — runs in a few seconds of wall time, roughly 8,600× faster
than real time.

### Against the researched criteria

| Benchmark | Source | Our number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| ~180 s per arcade credit | A-11, coin-op economics | wall 1 ≈ **310 s** | **72% over** |
| Casual session 3–7 min | modern retention research | full game **8.8–10 min** | **above top quartile** |
| First session must feel completable | retention research | beginner: **5 pts in 16 s** | **failing badly** |

### What this says about the brick count (Daniel's UX-22 question)

**A skilled player needs ~5.2 minutes to clear one wall, and ~9–10 minutes for the full
two-wall game.** That is roughly 40% longer than a top-quartile casual session and nearly
double a 1976 credit.

Cutting 8 rows to 6 (112 → 84 bricks, 336/wall) would take a wall to roughly **230 s** and
the full game to about **7 minutes** — landing on the casual top quartile and much nearer
the coin-op window.

**But the sim cannot answer the question that matters.** Daniel played the current build
and reported being *"tense the whole time"* and *"the speed was spot on."* A tense five
minutes is fine; a boring five minutes is not, and **time-to-clear cannot tell those
apart** (INV-13 — a metric that doesn't replicate the user's task doesn't validate it).
The sim measures length. Length is not the same thing as drag.

### The more actionable finding: it's the floor, not the ceiling

The beginner tier scores **5 points and is dead in 16 seconds**. Retention research is
consistent that early-session experience predicts everything downstream, and a 16-second
game over is a quit trigger. **If there is a problem here it is at the bottom of the skill
range, not the top** — which is the opposite of where the brick-count question was aimed.

---

## Honest limitations of this harness

**1. The tiers are not calibrated to humans.** The single real data point we have is
Daniel scoring **319**. The bots score **5** (beginner) and **826** (intermediate). The one
human we've measured falls in a gap the tiers don't bracket. n=1, but that 1 is worth more
than 30 bot runs, and right now the labels "beginner / intermediate / adept" are asserted,
not validated.

**2. Adept is superhuman.** 100% win rate, perfect 896 every single run, **zero balls lost
across 30 games.** No human plays like that. `adeptNotTrivial` fails as a check — a tier
with no failure mode isn't modelling a player, it's modelling an upper bound.

**3. Beginner may be modelling incompetence rather than inexperience.** It chases the
ball's current position with 260 ms lag and no prediction, which is close to hopeless by
construction. A real beginner reads the ball better than that. The 5-point median may be
the model failing, not the game.

**4. The first run silently reported the cap as a result.** `totalSec: 480` for both
stronger tiers was exactly the 8-minute limit — they were still playing when cut off. Now
raised to 25 minutes. A benchmark that quietly returns its own configuration is worse than
no benchmark, because it looks like data.

**5. It cannot measure fun, tension, readability, or frustration.** Every finding in this
project that mattered came from a human playing (INV-9). This harness exists to clear the
runway for that, not to replace it.

## Calibration plan

Each real playtest is one labelled data point. Record score, outcome and confidence, then
tune tier parameters until the bots bracket observed human results.

| Human | Score(s) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Daniel (experienced player) | **319** | *"tense the whole time"*; labels helped; speed felt right |
| Mum (casual) | **21** | first try |
| 9-year-old | **12, 13, 37** | visible learning curve across three attempts |
| 6-year-old | **4, 4, 5, 7** | four attempts |

## CALIBRATION RESULT — 2026-07-18

**The beginner tier was not miscalibrated. It was mislabelled at the top.**

The bot's beginner tier scored a median of **5**, range **4–7**. The 6-year-old scored
**4, 4, 5, 7**. That is not approximately right, it is *the same distribution*. The model
— chase the ball's current position, 260 ms lag, no prediction — turns out to be an
accurate portrait of an absolute first-timer.

What is wrong is everything above it:

| Tier | Bot median | Real human equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `beginner` | 5 | **6-year-old, first time.** Validated ✅ |
| *(gap — no tier)* | — | **9yo (12→37), Mum (21)** — genuine novices |
| *(gap — no tier)* | — | **Daniel, 319** — competent adult |
| `intermediate` | 826 | far beyond any human we measured |
| `adept` | 896 perfect, 0 balls lost | not a player; an upper bound |

**Proposed relabel + two new tiers**, so the ladder covers observed reality:

| New name | Anchor | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `firstTimer` | ~5 | 6yo — current `beginner`, keep parameters |
| `novice` | ~20–40 | 9yo / Mum — **needs building** |
| `competent` | ~300 | Daniel — **needs building** |
| `expert` | ~800 | current `intermediate`, relabelled |
| `ceiling` | 896 | current `adept` — not a player, a bound |

The three real anchors we now have (5 / ~25 / 319) are worth more than any number of bot
runs, and they say the tier ladder had a hole in the middle exactly where every actual
human lives.

**Note the learning curve too:** the 9-year-old went 12 → 13 → **37** across three tries.
Nearly 3× in three attempts. That is the strongest signal in the whole dataset that the
game teaches itself, and no bot tier models improvement at all — every simulated player
is frozen at one skill level forever. Worth building later: a tier whose parameters
improve across runs.
